strands.title: System Prompt Waste Analysis Project Scope & Phase Transition
strands.content: A system prompt analyzer module (src/pichay/analyzer.py) is being planned to measure waste and inefficiency in API calls across logged sessions. The core premise: system prompts are often reused verbatim, contain unused components, and have duplicated content—all representing wasted context tokens. The project focuses on instrumenting existing proxy JSONL logs to decompose prompts and measure waste quantitatively. This integrates into a larger pichay system already containing proxy.py (log capture) and eval.py (evaluation framework). The analyzer should produce structured results with dataclasses and expose functionality via CLI. **CYCLE 5 INTEGRATION: PRIORITY 1 task execution has begun. The [tool_result: Read] block has been received with the opening of proxy.py source code. This represents the transition from 'awaiting code content' to 'in receipt of code content and actively extracting findings.' The file header indicates proxy.py is a logging proxy for Claude API calls with two operational modes: Observe (default, logs request/response metrics, pure output) and presumably a second mode.** **CYCLE 6 INTEGRATION: A second [tool_use: Read] is present in new content, indicating user intends further code reading. However, NO NEW CODE CONTENT has been provided—only the tool_use marker. This represents a state of 'awaiting results from the read operation.' The tensor must track that: (1) a new read operation has been initiated, (2) results are not yet visible, (3) future content will likely contain code snippets from proxy.py or eval.py, (4) the system is in a 'pending read results' state. This cycle transitions from 'receiving code' to 'awaiting next code delivery.' The strand integrates this by noting the operational pause while maintaining PRIORITY 1 momentum.

key_claims.text: System prompts in API logs contain multiple distinct semantic components (tool defs, skill defs, CLAUDE.md, MEMORY.md, git status, IDE context, conversation instructions) that can be pattern-matched and decomposed
Most tool definitions are static across turns, while components like git status are dynamic; re-sending identical static components wastes tokens
A significant portion of tool definitions in system prompts are never invoked in actual tool_use blocks in messages
Skills and other semantic components appear multiple times (often tripled) within a single system prompt, representing duplicate waste
The project is now in operational execution phase with externally-persistent todo list; todos have been successfully created and are ready to be advanced
PRIORITY 1 task (read/analyze proxy.py and eval.py) has entered active execution phase and is in 'awaiting next read results' state at cycle 6

strands.title: Functional Requirements & Decomposition Strategy—Now Task-Backed
strands.content: The analyzer must implement five core analyses: (1) COMPONENT DECOMPOSITION—parse system prompt text and segment it by semantic block using pattern matching on text boundaries. (2) STATIC vs DYNAMIC ANALYSIS—track whether each component changes across turns within a session; measure bytes of static content re-sent unnecessarily. (3) TOOL USAGE TRACKING—extract tool definitions from system prompt, cross-reference against actual tool_use blocks in message records, identify defined-but-unused tools, and sum their byte cost. (4) DUPLICATE DETECTION—find identical or near-identical semantic blocks appearing multiple times in a single prompt; report byte counts. (5) PER-SESSION SUMMARY—aggregate metrics as absolute bytes and percentages. The module must use dataclasses to structure results, expose a CLI entry point, and require careful study of proxy.py and eval.py before implementation. **CYCLE 5-6 INTEGRATION: Code examination strategy remains active. After cycle 5's partial receipt of proxy.py header (lines 1-5), cycle 6 shows [tool_use: Read] marker without new code content, indicating the user is queuing another read operation. The tensor projects that either: (a) results from this read will arrive in a subsequent cycle, (b) the user is signaling intent to continue code examination, or (c) the read is a meta-signal to the tensor processor itself. The requirements remain valid and unchanged; implementation remains blocked pending full schema understanding. The functional decomposition strategy continues to be the framework guiding PRIORITY 1 and future PRIORITY 2-3 tasks.

key_claims.text: Component decomposition must rely on pattern matching over heuristic text segmentation to identify tool definitions, skill blocks, and metadata sections
Static vs dynamic analysis requires tracking component state across multiple turns within a session to measure re-transmission waste
Tool usage tracking depends on correctly parsing tool_use blocks in message JSON and matching their names against tool definitions in the system prompt
Duplicate detection should quantify byte cost of repeated semantic blocks, likely to reveal skills being tripled or other structural redundancy
Per-session summary metrics should report both absolute byte counts and percentages to allow comparative analysis across sessions and cost models

strands.title: Code Examination Phase 1-2 Transition: Partial Receipt and Await State
strands.content: In cycle 5, proxy.py code content was received (lines 1-5, partial): file header #!/usr/bin/env python3, docstring describing 'logging proxy for Claude API calls, with optional context paging,' and two operational modes identified: (1) Observe (default)—logs request/response metrics, produces pure output. A second mode was mentioned but not fully documented in the received excerpt. **CYCLE 6 TRANSITION**: The appearance of [tool_use: Read] in new content signals a continuation of the code examination workflow. However, NO NEW CODE HAS BEEN DELIVERED—only the tool marker. This indicates: (a) the read operation is queued/in-progress, (b) results are pending, (c) the tensor must track the 'await state,' (d) future cycles will likely provide results. The Phase 1 discovery (proxy.py header) remains valid. Phase 2 is now characterized as 'pending delivery'—the examination phase is not halted, but rather in a pause waiting for content. The open questions from cycle 5 remain unresolved: JSONL schema unknown, system_prompt_full location unknown, tool_use block structure unknown, eval.py not yet examined. The tensor must maintain these as live open questions rather than close them prematurely.

key_claims.text: proxy.py is a CLI-based logging proxy that captures Claude API calls for observation and analysis
proxy.py has at least two operational modes: Observe (default, logging-only) and one other (unnamed/not fully described in cycle 5 content)
In Observe mode, proxy.py logs request/response metrics without modifying or paging the context data
proxy.py is designed as an executable Python 3 script, suitable for CLI invocation from the command line
Cycle 6 shows [tool_use: Read] marker indicating a queued/in-progress read operation with results pending in future cycles

strands.title: Execution State & Task Momentum: Cycle 6 Status Hold
strands.content: PRIORITY 1 task remains active but in 'awaiting read results' state. Cycle 5 received initial proxy.py content (header/docstring); cycle 6 shows a read operation marker without delivered content. The system is in a controlled pause—not stalled, but waiting. The tensor tracks: (1) PRIORITY 1 status = 'in progress, awaiting next code delivery,' (2) code examination progress = partial (proxy.py header extracted; full file not yet received; eval.py not yet examined), (3) findings checkpoint = 'operational modes identified but schema details remain unknown,' (4) PRIORITY 2-3 remain blocked pending full PRIORITY 1 completion. The execution phase has established momentum (code is being read, examined, and tracked) but has not yet reached critical findings needed for downstream design work. The todo list from cycle 4-5 remains active and these findings represent progress checkpoints within PRIORITY 1. Cycle 7 will likely bring either: (a) completed proxy.py code, (b) eval.py code, or (c) a signal that code content is not forthcoming. The tensor must maintain readiness to pivot based on what arrives.

key_claims.text: PRIORITY 1 task is actively in-progress; code examination has begun but is not yet complete
Cycle 6 shows [tool_use: Read] marker indicating user intent to continue code reading/examination
No new code content has been delivered in cycle 6; results from the read operation are pending
The system is in 'awaiting state'—not stalled, but controlled pause pending next code delivery
PRIORITY 2-3 remain blocked pending full PRIORITY 1 completion and findings synthesis

open_questions: What is the complete source code of proxy.py, particularly the record schema (field names, types, nesting)?
Where exactly is system_prompt_full stored in proxy.py request records?
How are tool_use blocks structured in message JSON? What fields/naming conventions are used?
What is the second operational mode of proxy.py, and how does it differ from Observe mode?
What dataclass patterns and CLI design conventions are used in eval.py?
Does proxy.py support streaming/chunking of long prompts, and if so, how is that represented in the log format?
Are there any examples of actual JSONL log records in proxy.py or elsewhere in the codebase that would clarify the schema?
instructions_for_next: Cycle 7 should anticipate arrival of code content from either proxy.py (continuation) or eval.py (if proxy.py reading is complete). When code arrives: (1) extract all schema-relevant details (field names, types, nesting), (2) identify where system_prompt_full is located and how it's stored, (3) map tool_use block structure, (4) document findings incrementally in the appropriate strand, (5) cross-check findings against the five functional requirements to validate progress toward PRIORITY 1 completion. If no code arrives in cycle 7, consider whether user intent is to defer code reading or if a different conversational path is opening. The tensor should remain ready to pivot to requirements refinement, pseudo-code design, or other pre-implementation work if code examination is no longer available. Maintain the todo list as the ground truth for task priority and ordering.
